followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: First cycle; no prior review to compare.

main_claims.claim: The cosmological model (eqs. 5, 9) exactly reproduces all successes of cold dark matter including CMB anisotropies, BAO, and linear structure formation
main_claims.notes: This is logically watertight by construction: the stress tensor is defined to satisfy conservation identically, so it is dark matter by another name at linear order. The underlying derivation is in [15] (Deffayet & Woodard 2024) and is only reviewed here, not rederived. Correctness at the linear level is essentially definitional; the claim cannot fail within its stated scope.

main_claims.claim: The gravitationally bound model (eq. 28) reproduces BTFR and weak lensing in the static spherically symmetric limit
main_claims.notes: The derivation from BTFR to eq. (20) is straightforward algebra. The lensing consistency (Φ = Ψ) follows from the spatial Einstein equations in this geometry. The underlying model was validated in [11, 12]. No new validation is added here, but the logic of the reduction is presented and appears sound.

main_claims.claim: A single equation (eq. 33) interpolates between the cosmological and MOND regimes, recovering each in its respective limit
main_claims.notes: The cosmological limit (f(X) → 0 when X is large negative) is supported by the computation in Appendix 7. The recovery of the MOND limit is argued qualitatively: spatial dominance 'pushes F away from the homogeneous solution towards the MOND solution.' No quantitative demonstration of this transition is provided; Section 4.2 explicitly defers this to future numerical work. The interpolation is asserted, not proven.

main_claims.claim: The field-theory representation of the 4-velocity does not form caustics in a smooth static spherically symmetric geometry
main_claims.notes: Appendix 6 gives a clean recursive proof that all radial spatial derivatives of φ vanish at r=0 for the static case, preventing cusps there. However, the demonstration is restricted to a static geometry and does not cover dynamically evolving configurations relevant to structure formation or collapse. The paper acknowledges further studies are underway.

main_claims.claim: The model does not modify tensor mode propagation, satisfying multimessenger gravitational wave constraints
main_claims.notes: The cosmological sector argument relies on Barvinsky's mimetic gravity analysis [47]. The MOND sector argument is that the source contains two Ricci tensor factors, which vanish for gravitational radiation. This is plausible but the claim is stated briefly without a full perturbative analysis of the synthesized model. The nonlocal operator □⁻¹ acting on Ricci-tensor composites could in principle introduce subtle modifications at higher order.

main_claims.claim: The model contains no instabilities, ghosts, or runaway behaviors
main_claims.notes: Stability in each individual regime is cited from [47] (Barvinsky, mimetic gravity) and [14] (Tan & Woodard 2018, MOND perturbations). The synthesized model combining both has not been analyzed. Given the nonlocal, nonlinear coupling between the two sectors in the interpolating regime, this cannot be assumed to carry over without analysis.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Spatial metric gradients 'dominate' over time derivatives inside gravitationally bound systems in a way that makes eq. (33) select the MOND inhomogeneous solution over the cosmological homogeneous solution
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: This ordering assumption is the entire mechanism of the interpolation. If the cosmological homogeneous solution of F persists inside galaxies (e.g., because the transition is slow or incomplete), the MOND phenomenology would not be recovered and the synthesis would fail. No estimate of the relative magnitudes is given.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The nonlocal inverse d'Alembertian □⁻¹, defined with null initial data on the end-of-inflation surface, gives a unique and physically sensible result when applied inside a gravitationally bound system billions of years after inflation
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The prescription ties the nonlocal operator to initial conditions set at the end of inflation. This is an unusual and strong assumption. In practice, one must integrate eq. (27) from t_i to the present through cosmological history before reaching a galaxy. The sensitivity of X to this entire history, and whether the resulting value inside a galaxy has the right properties for MOND, is not demonstrated.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Nonlinear structure formation (from cosmology down to galactic binding) remains consistent with observations despite the interpolation mechanism potentially becoming active at intermediate scales
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The paper constructs the model to work at extreme limits (deep cosmology, deep MOND) but the transition region governs structure formation history. The lensing statistics, halo mass function, and matter power spectrum at nonlinear scales are all sensitive to this. The paper flags this as an open problem but no constraints are placed.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The stability results of Barvinsky [47] for mimetic gravity and Tan & Woodard [14] for the MOND model carry over to the synthesized model with the interpolating equation (33)
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Stability of individual limits does not guarantee stability of an interpolating theory. New instabilities or ghost modes can appear precisely at the transition between two otherwise stable configurations.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does reference [14] (Tan & Woodard 2018, JCAP) actually analyze perturbation stability for the model in Section 3 (gravitationally bound MOND) or only for the earlier class of models? The paper's claim that stability has been 'conducted with the same result' for the MOND model of Section 4 needs verification.
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: I cannot retrieve and verify the content of arXiv:1804.01669 to confirm whether its perturbative analysis covers the specific model defined by eqs. (27)-(30), or only an earlier variant.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the late-time behavior of X → 0 at z* ≈ 0 (Appendix 7, citing [13]) create any known problem for the background expansion or dark energy phenomenology? The paper is silent on this.
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires checking [13] (Kim et al. 2016, PRD) and understanding what numerical consequences the vanishing of X at late times has for the full model, including the MOND correction turning on cosmologically.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is there any prior literature that independently assesses whether the nonlocal Woodard-Deffayet MOND model [11,12] passes N-body or lensing statistics tests beyond the BTFR and weak lensing in isolated static systems?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: A literature search is needed to verify whether the gravitationally bound model has been tested beyond the two observables highlighted in the paper, especially at cluster scales or for disk galaxy diversity.

novelty_assessment: The paper's novelty is incremental but genuine. The cosmological model (Section 2) and the MOND model (Section 3) are both from prior publications by the same group ([15] and [11,12] respectively). The mimetic gravity Lagrangian (eq. 12) is from Chamseddine-Mukhanov [43,44]. The new contribution is: (1) the unified Lagrangian (eq. 31) and the single interpolating equation (eq. 33), (2) the qualitative argument for how the transition between regimes occurs, and (3) the observation that a single function f(X) can suppress the MOND term cosmologically while activating it in bound systems. This is a meaningful synthesis but is largely combinatorial of existing ingredients. The paper is honest about this. The field would benefit more from a follow-up paper that demonstrates the interpolation quantitatively; the present paper is best understood as establishing the theoretical framework and stating the interpolation conjecture.
score_rationale: Technical soundness is limited to 5 because the central new claim — that eq. (33) achieves a working interpolation — is heuristic and entirely undemonstrated; each individual regime is sound by prior construction but the synthesis has not been validated. Novelty is 5 because the paper connects two existing models from the same group through a natural but unsurprising mechanism; the ingredients are well-known and the combinatorial step, while correct, is not a major conceptual advance. Significance is 7 because if the model holds up, a single nonlocal gravity theory that unifies MOND and cosmological dark matter phenomenology without new particles would be a genuine result in modified gravity. Clarity is 7: the paper is well-structured, honest about limitations, and the mathematical steps are followable; the heavy reliance on unexplained equation numbering across regimes requires tracking context carefully. Reviewer confidence is 6: I can follow the algebraic structure and identify logical gaps, but cannot independently verify the numerical claims in the appendices or check the cited prior results without access to those papers.
summary: Deffayet and Woodard present a nonlocal modification of general relativity that unifies two prior successful models: (1) a cosmological model that exactly mimics cold dark matter by expressing its conserved stress tensor as a nonlocal functional of the metric, and (2) a MOND model for gravitationally bound systems that reproduces the Baryonic Tully-Fisher Relation and weak lensing. The synthesis is achieved through a single equation (eq. 33) for a nonlocal functional F, governed by an interpolating function f(X) where X is a Ricci-tensor-derived nonlocal invariant. In the cosmological regime X is large and negative, suppressing MOND contributions; in the static bound-system regime, spatial metric gradients dominate and the MOND solution is recovered. The paper is a synthesis paper built heavily on prior published work by the same group and collaborators. The main new contribution is the unified Lagrangian (eq. 31) and the interpolating equation (eq. 33), together with a qualitative argument for the transition mechanism. The authors are explicit that a quantitative demonstration of the transition and nonlinear phenomenology are deferred to follow-up work.

technical_objections.evidence: Section 4.2 explicitly states: 'a definitive analysis in a realistic setting is obviously a formidable numerical undertaking' and proposes only a fictitious interpolating geometry as a future follow-up. The paper's central claim — that the model interpolates correctly — therefore rests on an undemonstrated heuristic argument about which term in eq. (33) dominates in each regime.
technical_objections.objection: The claimed interpolation (eq. 33) is not demonstrated quantitatively. The paper asserts that spatial dominance 'pushes F toward the MOND solution' but provides no calculation, estimate, or even dimensional analysis for any realistic transition geometry.

technical_objections.evidence: The authors write that clusters 'are traditionally regarded as problematic for MOND' and suggest competition between the two solutions 'may' account for this. No calculation supports this. The Bullet Cluster in particular constrains theories with no explicit dark matter very tightly; a handwave here is not sufficient.
technical_objections.objection: Cluster-scale phenomenology (galactic clusters, Bullet Cluster) is acknowledged as problematic but entirely deferred. The speculation that 'competition between cosmological and MOND solutions' resolves cluster discrepancies is unsupported.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper states 'A similar analysis of perturbations for the MOND model of section 4 has been conducted with the same result [14].' Reference [14] is Tan & Woodard 2018 on the earlier MOND model, not the synthesized model of section 4.1. The interpolating equation (33) couples the two sectors and its perturbative stability has not been checked.
technical_objections.objection: Stability of the synthesized model (the interpolating regime) is not analyzed. The citations to [47] and [14] cover only the limiting cases (pure mimetic gravity and pure MOND), not the combined nonlocal system with the interpolating function f(X).

technical_objections.evidence: Equation (10) explicitly states 'the final equality exploits the numerical coincidence between [H₀] and Milgrom's constant a₀.' This coincidence is central to both regimes working simultaneously, yet the model treats it as an unexplained initial condition. The paper does not claim to derive it, but it is a significant underpinning of the synthesis claim.
technical_objections.objection: The numerical coincidence a₀ ≈ cH₀/2π is used in equation (10) as the initial condition magnitude but is not derived; it is an explicit fine-tuning input. The model has no mechanism to explain this coincidence.

technical_objections.evidence: The argument given is: 'its source [for tensor modes] contains two factors of the Ricci tensor, which vanishes for gravitational radiation.' This is correct at the local level but the nonlocal operator □⁻¹ in the definition of X (eq. 27) accumulates contributions from all past values of R_μν. A gravitational wave propagating through a matter distribution will have a nonzero X from background contributions, potentially modifying its own propagation. A more careful analysis is needed.
technical_objections.objection: The tensor mode argument for the MOND sector assumes that vanishing of the Ricci tensor for radiation implies the nonlocal source is inert, but does not analyze whether X (eq. 27), built from R_μν through the nonlocal operator □⁻¹, can carry memory of past matter distributions and affect tensor propagation nonlocally.

technical_objections.evidence: The paper states 'the accumulated positive contribution from early times is canceled to make X vanish' at late times. When X ≈ 0, f(X) is not negligible and the MOND correction is not suppressed. The paper cites [13] for a 'detailed numerical study' of this but does not present the implications for the background expansion or for the interpolation when both terms in eq. (33) are of comparable magnitude during late-time cosmological evolution.
technical_objections.objection: Appendix 7 shows X → 0 at a finite redshift z* ≈ 0 during late-time cosmology. This means the interpolating function f(X) is not permanently suppressed in the cosmological regime; the cosmological-to-MOND boundary becomes relevant at late times even for the background, creating a potential tension with late-time cosmology.
